our_argument_summary: Our review says an R² of 0.79 means 21% of variance is unexplained, that standard geodesic calculations (Vincenty 1975) achieve sub-0.5% error (R² > 0.995), and that adding free parameters (quadratic term) always increases R² without AIC/BIC correction for overfitting.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The dome model claims to have measured 20 flight/shipping routes with wind corrections and computed the ratio of dome-predicted distance to actual distance for each route. These ratios are then fit to a quadratic polynomial in latitude: ratio = 0.00131*lat² + 0.06828*lat + 1.06719. However, NO script in the repository actually performs this quadratic regression. find_curve.py contains a skeleton with 8 city coordinates and 6 hardcoded real distances, plus an incomplete calc_r_v506() function (literally contains 'pass'). verify_v51.py computes Euclidean distances on the disc but does not perform polynomial regression. The R²=0.7874 value and the quadratic coefficients appear to be asserted results with no reproducible computation in the codebase.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The quadratic coefficients (0.00131, 0.06828, 1.06719) and R²=0.7874 are presented as results but no code in the repository computes them. The 20 route pairs and wind corrections are not documented in any data file. find_curve.py has only 6 hardcoded city-pair distances. The '5.2% mean error' claim appears in the raw text notes but is also not computed by any script.
data_sources_used: No external data sources fetched for this WIN — all values appear to be static assertions
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The model evolution note states 'v22 found circular disc fails on southern/cross-equator distances. That was correct.' This is an admission that the model failed and was then patched. The quadratic law and elliptical geometry (WIN-030) are post-hoc corrections added after the failure was identified, not predictions.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not make specific claims about the dome's code for WIN-027. This is actually a gap — we could strengthen our argument by noting that the quadratic fit is completely unreproducible from the repository's own code.

holes_found.description: Our review cites Vincenty (1975) DOI 10.1179/sre.1975.23.176.88 for 'sub-0.5% error'. This is technically accurate but slightly imprecise: Vincenty's formula achieves sub-millimeter accuracy (0.5 mm on WGS84), not merely 'sub-0.5% error'. The R² > 0.995 comparison is correct in spirit — globe geodesic predictions have essentially zero unexplained variance — but our review doesn't specify what the 'error' metric is for (we imply R² but Vincenty is usually described in absolute distance terms). A smart defender could argue we're comparing apples to oranges: the dome's R² measures variance in a distance *ratio* as a function of latitude, not raw distance error.
holes_found.recommendation: Clarify that the globe model's 'error' is in raw distance prediction (sub-0.01% for Vincenty on WGS84, which is sub-millimeter per kilometer), and that comparing this to the dome's R²=0.79 for distance ratios is actually even MORE devastating: the globe doesn't need a latitude-dependent correction factor at all, so the 'ratio' would be 1.000 everywhere (R² is undefined because there's no variance to explain).

holes_found.description: Our review says 'Adding free parameters (quadratic term) always increases R²' — this is technically true only when adding parameters to a polynomial regression of the SAME data. A defender could argue the quadratic was discovered as a real pattern, not merely the result of overfitting. However, with only 20 data points and 3 free parameters, and an R² of only 0.79, this argument is weak.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding: 'With 3 free parameters and only 20 data points, the adjusted R² would be even lower. More critically, the quadratic correction is applied AFTER the base model was found to fail by ~40%, making this a post-hoc patch rather than a prediction.'

holes_found.description: Our review doesn't mention what the 'ratio' in the quadratic actually means. If it's the ratio of dome-predicted distance to actual distance, then the dome model is admitting its base geometry DOESN'T predict correct distances — it needs a latitude-dependent fudge factor. This is a much more powerful argument than we currently make.
holes_found.recommendation: Add explicit statement: 'The existence of a latitude-dependent distance ratio is itself an admission that the disc geometry fails to predict correct distances. The quadratic law is a correction factor for a failing model, not a prediction.'

stronger_arguments.description: The quadratic law is the dome model's own admission of failure. A correct geometric model would predict distances directly — it wouldn't need a latitude-dependent correction factor. The globe model predicts every distance on Earth to sub-millimeter accuracy using a single set of WGS84 parameters (semi-major axis, flattening). The dome model, by contrast, needs the base disc geometry PLUS an exponential height function H(r) PLUS an elliptical correction (WIN-030) PLUS this quadratic latitude correction — and STILL has 21% unexplained variance. Each added correction is a free parameter absorbing error from the failing base model.

The dome's codebase has no reproducible implementation of this WIN. find_curve.py contains a skeleton with an empty function (literally 'pass'). No script performs the quadratic regression. No data file contains the 20 routes or wind corrections. This makes the R²=0.7874 an unverifiable assertion — it cannot be reproduced from the repository's own code, similar to the 95.2% accuracy issue (WIN hardcoded in HTML). A scientific prediction that can't be reproduced from published methods is not a prediction at all.

The Sydney-Santiago flight time directly refutes the disc model's distance predictions. On a flat disc, Sydney to Santiago would be ~30,000+ km (nearly across the entire disc). The actual great-circle distance is ~11,340 km, and flights take ~12 hours 40 minutes — consistent with globe geometry but impossible at subsonic speeds on a flat disc. No quadratic correction factor can fix a 3x distance discrepancy.

The model evolution note in the raw text is damning: 'v22 found circular disc fails on southern/cross-equator distances. That was correct. WIN-027 (quadratic law) and WIN-030 (ellipse) directly address this.' This explicitly admits (1) the model failed, (2) the failure was in distance predictions, and (3) WIN-027 was created to 'address' (i.e., patch) the failure. This is the opposite of a prediction — it's a post-hoc parameter adjustment. In real science, discovering your model fails and then adding correction factors is called 'fitting', not 'confirming'.

kernel_of_truth: The dome model correctly identifies that projecting a sphere onto a flat disc introduces systematic, latitude-dependent distance distortions. This is real cartography — it's exactly what the azimuthal equidistant projection does, and the distortions DO follow a roughly quadratic pattern at mid-latitudes. The kernel of genuine science here is map projection theory. The irony is that the dome model has independently rediscovered that flat maps distort distances in the southern hemisphere — which is precisely the evidence that the Earth is NOT flat. The quadratic distance correction factor the dome needs is mathematically equivalent to compensating for the curvature it denies exists.
deeper_analysis:
WIN-027 represents one of the most revealing entries in the entire dome model, because it contains an explicit admission that the model's core geometric prediction — distances between cities — fails without post-hoc correction. The claim is that 20 flight/shipping routes show a quadratic relationship between latitude and the ratio of predicted-to-actual distances, with R²=0.7874 and coefficients 0.00131*lat² + 0.06828*lat + 1.06719.

Our current review correctly identifies the two main problems: the R² of 0.79 leaves 21% unexplained variance, and no AIC/BIC comparison is presented. However, our review could be significantly strengthened by emphasizing a more fundamental point: the existence of a latitude-dependent distance ratio is itself proof that the disc geometry is wrong. A geometrically correct model doesn't need correction factors. The standard geodesic model (Vincenty 1975, refined by Karney 2013 to sub-nanometer accuracy) predicts every point-to-point distance on Earth using exactly two parameters (semi-major axis 6,378,137 m and flattening 1/298.257223563) with errors measured in millimeters. The dome model needs its base disc radius, an exponential firmament height function, an elliptical axis ratio, AND a quadratic latitude correction — and still fails on 21% of the variance.

A critical finding from the code analysis is that the R²=0.7874 value is unreproducible from the repository's own source code. The file find_curve.py contains only a skeleton with 8 cities, 6 hardcoded distances, and an empty function (literally 'pass' as the body). No script in the repository performs the claimed quadratic regression on 20 routes. No data file contains the 20 route definitions or wind corrections. This makes WIN-027 another instance of an unverifiable assertion, paralleling the 95.2% accuracy issue where the number was hardcoded in HTML with no backing computation.

The dome model's own evolution notes are remarkably candid about what happened: 'v22 found circular disc fails on southern/cross-equator distances. That was correct. WIN-027 (quadratic law) and WIN-030 (ellipse) directly address this.' This is an explicit admission of the sequence: model predicted distances → distances were wrong → correction factors were added → corrections are now called 'confirmed predictions.' In standard scientific methodology, this is post-hoc curve fitting, not prediction.

Perhaps most devastatingly, the quadratic correction the dome model needs is mathematically equivalent to compensating for spherical geometry it denies exists. The azimuthal equidistant projection (the standard flat-earth map) distorts east-west distances by a factor of 1/sinc(θ) where θ is the colatitude — which is well-approximated by a quadratic at mid-latitudes. The dome model has independently rediscovered that its flat map distorts distances in exactly the way a sphere-to-plane projection predicts. The 'quadratic latitude law' IS the curvature of the Earth, expressed as a correction factor on a flat map.
